rules.title: Repository function must contain exactly one database call
rules.description: Each repository function must perform exactly one database operation. Multiple DB calls in a single repository function blur responsibilities, make transactions ambiguous, and hide N+1 patterns. Split the function or wrap the calls in a single transaction helper.
rules.suggested_fix: Split this function into multiple single-responsibility repository functions, one per DB operation. If the operations are logically atomic, wrap them in a single database transaction and issue the calls through the transaction handle inside a dedicated helper.

rules.title: Database call must not appear inside a for/range loop (N+1 anti-pattern)
rules.description: Issuing a database call inside a loop produces N round-trips for N iterations (the N+1 query problem). This causes exponential latency growth and connection-pool exhaustion under load. Batch the identifiers and issue a single bulk query instead.
rules.suggested_fix: Collect all required identifiers first, then issue a single bulk query using IN ($1, $2, ...) or pgx SendBatch. Alternatively, use a JOIN or a CTE to retrieve all related data in one round-trip.

rules.title: Handler must not call more than one repository/service method directly
rules.description: HTTP handlers should be thin adapters. Orchestrating multiple repository calls directly in a handler embeds business logic in the transport layer, prevents reuse, complicates testing, and leaks domain concerns. Introduce a service/use-case layer to own the multi-repo orchestration.
rules.suggested_fix: Introduce a service / use-case layer (e.g. UserService.CreateWithProfile) that encapsulates the multi-repository orchestration. The handler calls exactly one service method; the service method calls as many repositories as needed, managing transactions internally.
